items.text: Wash hands and don PPE if appropriate
Introduce yourself to the patient including your name and role
Confirm the patient's name and date of birth
Explain that you would like to take a history from the patient
Gain consent to proceed with taking a history
Use open questions to explore the presenting complaint and facilitate the patient to expand
Explore the presenting complaint using SOCRATES (site, onset, character, radiation, associated symptoms, time course, exacerbating and relieving factors, severity)
Screen for key respiratory symptoms (dyspnoea, cough, haemoptysis, wheeze, chest pain, weight loss)
Explore the patient's ideas, concerns and expectations (ICE)
Summarise the patient's presenting complaint
Screen for relevant symptoms in other body systems
Ask about medical conditions including pre-existing respiratory disease (asthma, COPD) and previous respiratory infections
Ask about previous surgeries or procedures (lobectomy, bronchoscopy)
Ask about other medical diagnoses and previous surgical history
Ask about vaccinations against respiratory diseases (influenza, pneumococcus, COVID-19, tuberculosis)
Ask about prescribed medications and over-the-counter remedies
Ask about allergies and the type of reaction
Ask about family history of respiratory disease and age of onset in relatives
Explore general social context (accommodation, who lives with, ability to manage ADLs)
Take a smoking history
Take an alcohol history
Ask about recreational drug use
Ask about exposure to allergens (animal dander, pollen, dust)
Ask about occupation (past and present) for exposure to pulmonary irritants or hazardous substances (e.g. asbestos)
Take a travel history if an infective aetiology is suspected (e.g. tuberculosis)
Ask about hobbies (e.g. bird keeping, caving)
Summarise the key points of the history
Ask if the patient has any questions or concerns not yet addressed
Thank the patient for their time
Dispose of PPE appropriately and wash hands
Active listening (reflecting, not interrupting, showing empathy)
Summarising (summarising back what the patient has told you)
Signposting (informing the patient what you are about to discuss next)
